name: big_endian_s16_cast
title: (s16)v emits lh +2 on PSX (reads HIGH halfword), not the LOW one
summary:
GCC 2.7.2 treats `(s16)v` casts via memory-aligned big-endian semantics: when a 32-bit value is in memory and accessed as s16, GCC emits `lh ofs+2` (reads the BIG-endian high halfword). On PSX little-endian, `+2` is the HIGH 16 bits of the word, not the low ones. Workers expecting `(s16)v` to read the LOW 16 bits get bizarre regressions.

Fix: swap the C semantics. If you want the low 16 bits of v, write `v >> 16` (which emits `srl ofs`). If you want the high 16 bits, use `(s16)v` (which emits `lh ofs+2`).
proof: func_8007FBBC (worktree-agent-a37336621adaa378d) had wrong sin/cos halves until the worker swapped `cos = v >> 16; sin = (s16)v` to `cos = (s16)v; sin = v >> 16`.

structural: C source uses (s16) cast on a 32-bit value (or struct field)
diff shows lh with offset+2 in target where mine has lh with offset (or vice versa)
diff also shows wrong halfword extracted into the working register

rule_skeleton.instruction: Audit each (s16)/(u16) cast. If target's lh has +2 offset, the cast IS reading the high halfword -- match accordingly. If target's lh has +0 offset, swap your semantics: use `>> 16` instead of (s16).

notes: Quick test: read target asm for the lh instruction; the `+2` (or absence of) tells you which halfword is wanted.
Common in fixed-point math (sin/cos tables, matrix entries packed as s16:s16).
